The honest finding is that this is a real gap, but the design memo calls it 0.9.0-α work (~700 LOC + 500 tests, line 197). The design's response is a `~100 LOC recovery primitive` (§1, line 22) and per-migration declared mappings. Nothing in this category needs work right now because no migration since the framework discussion has needed backfill — Phase 5A's `derived_from` add is lossless (additive column). Don't build the primitive on spec; build it the first time a migration actually needs it. ## Surgical recommendations (prioritized by value/effort ratio) 1. **Test-isolation audit + `BUN_TEST` write-guard** — 1 day. Reduces wasted reflect/distill spend on low-confidence assets. Specifications already in `plan.md:85-101`. 7. **`VACUUM INTO` backup (Subsystem A) — partial** — 2 days for the snapshot + verify + sidecar `.meta.json`, leave the CLI subcommands (`akm db restore`, `prune`) for later. The 11 distinct `broken_frontmatter_*` / `Malformed frontmatter` / "section heading not a description" reasons (`/home/founder3/akm/.akm/proposals/archive/4288bab4-a9b8-40ad-81a9-69c9e348995c/proposal.json:20`, `725d5404-908a-4f5e-ba2d-f63543158ff9/proposal.json:20`, `0e81d6e4-774b-472f-a771-697d99237dcb/proposal.json:20`, `8994fa4a-e9fe-460d-868c-ea9151b30c2d/proposal.json:19`) are the post-hoc descriptions of the failure modes the new validators were built to catch. ### Key calibration up front The hypothesis stated "Distill recursive-lesson guard skips 409 inputs every hourly run — that's a real win because 409 broken proposals/run is huge." **This is wrong.** Counting `recursive_lesson_input` in the runs directory yields 16 hits across 30 runs — i.e. **0 or 2 per run** (`/home/founder3/akm/.akm/runs/*` count). The 409-ish number that looks compelling on first glance is *actions per run*, of which 99%+ are `reflect cooldown (pre-filtered)` skips — completely unrelated to the recursive-lesson guard. This recalibration matters for the recommendations below. ## Validators that earn their keep ### Tier 1 — directly catching observed failure modes - **`sanitizeReflectPayload` + `PROTECTED_FRONTMATTER_FIELDS`** (`src/commands/reflect.ts:573`, `:243`). Defends against the exact regression in archived proposal `8994fa4a-e9fe-460d-868c-ea9151b30c2d` ("Removes entire YAML frontmatter (description, when_to_use, updated).") and `65a88e1a-169e-461b-9983-fd48720a1760` (same root cause). Two distinct reflect runs had this exact failure — the safety rail is well-targeted. **Keep.** - **Reflect expand rail (`REFLECT_EXPAND_RATIO_MAX = 2.0`)** (`src/commands/reflect.ts:247`, fires at `:638`). Fired 2× in the 17-run sample (`/home/founder3/akm/.akm/runs/2026-05-20T03-08-18-618Z-bf899f37/improve-result.json:3307` and `/home/founder3/akm/.akm/runs/2026-05-20T02-08-27-280Z-09839cc3/improve-result.json` — `EXCESSIVE_EXPANSION — proposed body is 264% of source` on `knowledge:policies/lightweight-registry`). That's ~1 catch per 8 runs, against a real LLM tendency to balloon short policies into essays. **Keep.** - **`sanitizeMergedContent` / `stripOuterCodeFence` / `UNBALANCED_CODE_FENCE`** (`src/commands/consolidate.ts:1249`, `:1224`). Fired in 4 of 17 runs against `memory:akm-consolidation-dedup-may-2026` (`/home/founder3/akm/.akm/runs/2026-05-20T04-07-15-874Z-547610e8/improve-result.json:3496`, etc.). This is the "outer ```yaml fence" defect from the archived `764e6c71-d676-4d3b-9a65-966bd0922f87/proposal.json:16` case. **Keep.** - **Content-hash dedup of pending consolidate proposals** (`src/commands/consolidate.ts:1027-1039`). Fires 29 times across 8 of 17 runs (`Skipping promote: identical content already pending`). Directly prevents the 103 archived "duplicate from concurrent runs" proposals from continuing to accumulate. **Keep.** (Note: this is the SHA-256 check at `:1031`, NOT `checkPreEmitDedup`. See below — those are distinct layers.) - **Distill recursive-lesson guard** (`src/commands/distill.ts:847-867`). Fires ~2× per run when it fires, in roughly 8 of 30 runs. The archived `187de1c9-d7eb-47c1-92a2-23ad29f669cc/proposal.json:20` (`"double-suffix 'lesson-...-lesson-lesson' ref naming"`) is the exact failure being prevented. Low volume but cheap-to-evaluate and there's a clear historical defect being killed. **Keep, but downgrade its prominence in the comments** (currently described as catching "323 archived rejected proposals" — that number isn't anchored to recursive-lesson alone, it's the union across many distill defects). ### Tier 2 — defensive but unverifiable in the sample - **`detectDoubleFrontmatter`** (`src/commands/distill.ts:417`). Archived `4288bab4-a9b8-40ad-81a9-69c9e348995c/proposal.json:9` shows the exact `**description:** ...` pseudo-frontmatter-in-body defect. It hasn't fired in the 17-run sample (because distill itself is at near-zero volume — only 1 of 417 actions in the most recent run was a real distill, all others were cooldown-skipped). But the prompt-rule it backstops *also* tightened in the same change, so silence here is likely deterrence working. **Keep, but track via a counter** — see "Log/event noise" below. ## Validators that may not earn their keep ### `checkPreEmitDedup` slug/embedding branches (`src/commands/consolidate.ts:1439-1542`) Of all the new helpers, this is the highest-cost / lowest-observed-value addition. - **What it does**: three checks per promote — (1) `normalizeSlugForDedup` match against every knowledge+memory entry in the DB, (2) slug-normalize match against pending consolidate proposals, (3) `embed()` the candidate + `embedBatch()` ALL knowledge+memory entries and check cosine ≥ 0.85. - **What actually fires in production**: zero slug-variant matches and zero cosine matches across 30 runs (`grep -c "slug-variant of"` returns 0; `grep -c "cosine similarity"` returns 0). The 29 dedup catches you see in run logs all come from the **separate** content-hash check at `:1027-1039`, which is much cheaper. - **Cost**: One `embed()` call + an `embedBatch()` over all knowledge AND non-derived memory (`:1493-1498`) **per promote op**. There are two important correctness issues and several minor ones. --- ### Detailed Findings --- **[SEVERITY: Important] Index-keyed `{#each}` causes stale-expansion after removal** - **Location**: Lines 461, 468, 538, 564, 572, 613 - **Issue**: Both `{#each llmProfiles as p, i (i)}` and `{#each agentProfiles as p, i (i)}` use the array index as the Svelte key. When a profile at index `j < expandedLlmIdx` is removed, `llmProfiles.filter(...)` produces a new array where every item shifts down by one. The `expandedLlmIdx` is only cleared when the removed item is exactly the expanded one (line 468: `if (expandedLlmIdx === i) expandedLlmIdx = null`). It is not decremented. So if profile 0 is removed while profile 1 is expanded (`expandedLlmIdx === 1`), after removal the old profile 1 is now at index 0, but `expandedLlmIdx` is still `1` — which now refers to a different profile or is out of bounds. With index-keyed `{#each}`, Svelte reuses DOM nodes by position, not by identity. After removal, the node previously at index 1 is now rendered at index 0, but Svelte considers the key `0` a new node (the old key `0` was destroyed) and key `1` either remounts or is gone. This does not cause a crash, but the UI will show the wrong card as expanded, or show no card expanded when one should be. The remove handler for agent profiles (line 572) has the same problem. - **Impact**: The expanded card visually jumps to the wrong profile after any removal of a card that precedes the expanded one. This is a user-visible correctness bug under normal usage patterns. - **Recommendation**: After filtering, adjust the expanded index: ```js // LLM remove (line 468) onclick={() => { if (expandedLlmIdx === i) { expandedLlmIdx = null; } else if (expandedLlmIdx !== null && expandedLlmIdx > i) { expandedLlmIdx = expandedLlmIdx - 1; } llmProfiles = llmProfiles.filter((_, j) => j !== i); }} // Agent remove (line 572) — same pattern ``` Alternatively, switch to stable IDs. Then the expanded-index approach can also be replaced with `expandedLlmId: string | null`. This is the more robust fix and also prevents the DOM-reuse issue described next. --- **[SEVERITY: Important] `{#each ... (i)}` index key causes DOM reuse on removal, breaking uncontrolled input state** - **Location**: Lines 461, 564 - **Issue**: When items are removed from the middle of the list, index-based keying tells Svelte that the node at position `i` represents the "same" element as whatever was at `i` before the update. For controlled inputs (`bind:value`), this is safe because the bound value dictates the displayed value. However, if the browser has any in-flight IME composition, browser autofill, or the `type="url"` input has a pending validation tooltip attached to a specific DOM node, those will shift to the wrong profile card. More practically: if a user removes profile 0 while profile 1 is mounted in the DOM, Svelte moves the DOM node that was profile 1 into position 0 and destroys the node at position 1 (not position 0). With `bind:value` in place, the reactive value will be correct — but this is purely because `bind:value` overwrites the DOM node's `.value`. It is not a semantic match. The stronger argument for stable IDs is maintainability: any future addition of uncontrolled state inside the card (a file input, a drag-over flag, a per-card error state) will silently get attached to the wrong card after removal. - **Impact**: Currently low risk because all inputs are `bind:value`-controlled.
